content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Calibration: Prompting LLMs with Uncertainty-Aware Contrastive Queries",
  "problem": "Current LLM prompting methods for uncertainty quantification rely on indirect proxies, such as softmax probabilities or self-reported confidence, which are often miscalibrated. There is a lack of prompt-based mechanisms that directly elicit and calibrate reliable uncertainty estimates from the model.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs see widespread deployment in high-stakes applications, accurately quantifying and calibrating their uncertainty is essential for downstream safety and trust. Existing approaches either interrogate the model in a single direction or require expensive ensembles. Prompting LLMs with structured, uncertainty-focused queries can unlock richer self-assessment at much lower overhead.",
  "method": "We propose a novel prompting technique that presents the LLM with contrastive, counterfactual queries: for each input, the model is asked to generate both the answer and a plausible alternative, accompanied by a natural-language explanation of uncertainty. By prompting for counterfactuals and rationales explicitly, we extract both model preference and epistemic uncertainty signals. The method can be generalized to n-way contrastive prompts and is compatible with few-shot and chain-of-thought settings. Calibration can then be learned by mapping these contrastive likelihoods or rationales to calibrated uncertainty scores.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on QA datasets (e.g., SQuAD, TriviaQA, TruthfulQA) and calibration benchmarks. Baselines include vanilla prompting, self-reported confidence, and existing uncertainty quantification techniques. Metrics will include Brier score, ECE, and task accuracy; human judgment will be used to evaluate explanation plausibility. We will also analyze calibration under distribution shifts."
}
